summary: I was born in a small town in Poland called Gonitz. After World War I, when I was about twenty-one, I was called to serve in the Polish Army, but my wife didn't want me to go. Life in Poland was hard, especially after the war, and my wife wrote me letters telling me how terrible it was there. I couldn't go back, especially after one of our children died, so I tried to bring my family to America.

Two years after World War I, I was called to serve in the Polish Army. My wife was against it and urged me to try to go to America instead. I was actually running away from military service, which made it impossible to get a passport. That decision set me on a difficult path to escape Poland and eventually reach the United States.

I crossed into Germany on a cold January night by walking through the woods with two other men. After paying fifteen marks to the guide, we waited until morning and then took a wagon to Berlin. Germany was just the first step on my long journey from Poland to America.

After entering Germany, we took a wagon to Berlin and then caught a train to Amsterdam. Berlin was a brief stop on my way out of Europe, but it was an important part of the route that eventually led me to America. From there, the journey continued through Holland and beyond.

From Berlin, I took a train to Amsterdam in Holland. Amsterdam was where I boarded a ship bound for Cuba. It was one of the key transit points on my way to America, although the real challenges began once I reached Cuba.

Holland was part of my journey when I traveled from Berlin to Amsterdam. It was there that I boarded a boat to Cuba, marking the start of a very difficult leg of my trip. Holland was a stepping stone on my way from Europe to the Americas.

I arrived in Cuba after leaving Holland, and that's where the real excitement and hardship began. I stayed in a boarding house sharing a room with two other men while I tried to find a way to get to Chicago. After six weeks, I paid a man $150 to smuggle me into America, which involved a terrifying night crossing by boat to Key West.

Chicago was my final destination in America, where I had three brothers and many relatives. When I arrived, I was so overwhelmed that I cried like a baby. My brother helped me start selling fish, which I have done ever since. Although I faced many challenges, including not being able to bring my family at first, Chicago became my new home.

After a grueling and cramped boat ride from Cuba, we finally reached Key West, Florida, late one night. We were exhausted and nearly dead from thirst and heat, but the man who smuggled us helped wash our faces and gave us some water. We stayed at a dirty boarding house near the ocean before continuing our journey by train.

From Key West, we took a train to Jacksonville, Florida, where we waited for six hours before boarding another train to Chicago. Jacksonville was just a brief stop on my long journey to reunite with my family. It was part of the route that finally brought me to my new life in America.

I came to the United States after a long and difficult journey through Europe, Cuba, and Florida. I arrived in Chicago where my brothers lived, and I started selling fish to make a living. At first, I couldn't get citizenship because I was smuggled in, which meant I couldn't bring my wife and children. Eventually, a law passed that allowed me to get my papers and reunite my family here.

After struggling for years without citizenship because I was smuggled into the United States, a law was passed that allowed people who came before 1921 to get their citizenship papers. This law was a turning point for me because I was able to get my papers and bring my wife and daughter to Chicago. It finally gave me some peace after all the hardships I endured.
